moment_text: Yeah, there's been many different times in my life where I've had like shifts that really, you know, change me. But I have to say, the one that really started me on the path that I'm on today is when that came after I kind of hit rock bottom. So I had struggled with my body image and my weight, my, my whole life since I was a a small child. And when I was. 24, I had a gastric bypass surgery. I had my daughter and then I gained a bunch of weight back. I hit rock bottom. I just, you know, I was self-destructive. It was just really bad. But at that bottom, I knew that I didn't need another diet or another. Like I knew I needed to love and accept myself.
eligibility_rationale: This moment contains strong emotional signals (hopelessness) and describes a significant lived experience of hitting a turning point after a medical intervention, which is a key element for pattern identification. It meets gate A2.

moment_text: Like I knew that that was what I needed to do. So I started working with a life coach and I read Ecker Tolle's, a New Earth, uh, which is all about presence and being the moment. I, there was one session with my life coach, although, you know, it's a built up. It's not really one where there was like the shift inside me where I felt like I broke my self-worth, apart from my physical body or anything else. I would often say prior to this that I, I can't be who I really am until I lose weight. It's stopping me from being, having my full potential. And when I realized that I was whole. Because I was connected to the real me and not the ego, not the voice in my head. That's when my life really started to transform.
eligibility_rationale: The moment details a clear realization and the agency taken to get there (seeking support), which is a valuable pattern for understanding protective factors. It meets gate A2.
notes_for_reviewer: The system interaction is tagged as 'peer_community' because a life coach is not a licensed clinician, making 'healthcare_provider' inappropriate.

moment_text: The first thing that happened, I was able to take care of my body in a way that I had never, that had felt like a chore before I started exercising and, and eating well and all that stuff. And it, it felt like magic because all the times I had tried to do it because I hated myself. You know, it didn't really work. And, um, but when I. Started loving myself. It became natural.
eligibility_rationale: This moment describes a clear behavioral pattern shift, linking emotional state (self-love vs. self-hate) to health behaviors, making it highly eligible for insight generation. It meets gate A2.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker's previous state of 'hating myself' could be seen as a barrier of 'self_blame', but the moment focuses on the positive shift, so I left barrier_signals empty.

moment_text: All this space was freed up in my mind. To be able to, to do so much more. And it's really, the path has continued because it's been evolution because like the, the journey of self-love and acceptance, it like started there. But over the years I've gone deeper and deeper into loving myself and having grace for myself, and I deeply believe that. That is why I'm able to do the leadership, to lead in the way that I do in social justice now because, uh, you know, I don't, you know, not that I don't have feelings of imposter syndrome and, and feeling, you know, all the feelings that humans do. But I have the, the ability to see that and step away from it instead of be caught up in it all the time and able to step in and step into my power and who I am and my capacity and my skills, and do what I can do.
eligibility_rationale: This moment connects an internal protective factor (self-love) to external action and leadership, which is a significant pattern. It contains both emotional and agency signals and a system interaction, meeting gate A2.

moment_text: Practice when you first start to understand like your triggers or understand like where, when you're being emotionally triggered. See it coming, it, I felt like it was like a, a truck coming at me and, and I couldn't stop it. I went full emotional dysregulation, crazy person. But over time you learn how to like see things and it's not always a hundred percent, but the more you practice and the more you know that those voices in your head that or, or that trigger, what that trigger is, the more that they lose as much power.

moment_text: The self-love, you know, came when I realized that I, it when, when it really became unconditional. But the truth is like accepting, you know, and, and giving yourself grace.

moment_text: And also just realizing like all this stuff is like, doesn't even matter. There's so many things about our bodies that like, no, these little things that we don't like. moment_text: The, the sh the, the shift. And then what happened with the fitness is what is, I decided I wanted to live in my body, like I wanted to be in my body. moment_text: And I was like. I can't put this kid in the hospital again 'cause it was so traumatic the last time. So I said, let's go to the Poconos for a weekend. moment_text: That summer 2020, we did, you know, lots of different protests and at the end of the summer we decided that we were gonna cap off the summer by doing protests across the county and do a car parade in between. And so we said Community for change, and that was just the name of that, that big action that we did. But then we continued, we continued to do educational events and voting things and the trans rates, rallies and all kinds of stuff, even through COVID and lockdown and things like that. And it's always been about coalition and community. Like there's, there's lots of helpers out there, there's lots of different activists and organizations, and we tend to be kind of in our own silos. Like bringing people together to connect to find that common ground. So that's always been part of it. And then, you know, more recently this year with what's happening in the country, the election we've been, we started to get activated again and, and what And but, but now it's even diff it's different. 'cause we're doing a lot of activism and we're doing a lot of resistance. We do protests, but we're building community. From a lot of people's lives in our society, we've forgotten how to rely on each other and how to work with each other.

moment_text: The first week after the election, I wasn't like sure what to do. Like do I start running like women's circles again or healing circles for people? And she's, we decided you're, let's stop trying to figure it out. And I swear that night I had like an epiphany because I, I, it hit me. I can't control what happens at the top. I can't control the federal government. I can't control what's happening in the world. But what I can do is I control how I act my values. And what I believe in, in my relationship with my neighbors, in the relationships with my family, with the relationships with my community, and ultimately the relationship with myself.
